flashcards.front: What is Randomized Select also known as?
flashcards.back: Randomized Select is also known as QuickSelect or Hoare's Selection Algorithm.

flashcards.front: What is the primary purpose of Randomized Select?
flashcards.back: To find the k-th smallest (or largest) element in an unordered list without fully sorting the array.

flashcards.front: What is the expected time complexity of Randomized Select?
flashcards.back: O(n) - linear time on average, making it highly efficient for selection problems.

flashcards.front: What is the worst-case time complexity of Randomized Select?
flashcards.back: O(n²) - occurs when the pivot selection consistently results in highly unbalanced partitions.

flashcards.front: Why does Randomized Select use a random pivot?
flashcards.back: To avoid worst-case scenarios and ensure expected O(n) performance by minimizing the probability of consistently bad pivot choices.

flashcards.front: How does Randomized Select differ from QuickSort?
flashcards.back: QuickSort recursively sorts both partitions while Randomized Select only recurses into one partition (the one containing the k-th element), saving half the work.

flashcards.front: What is the key operation in Randomized Select?
flashcards.back: Partitioning - the same procedure used in QuickSort that divides the array around a pivot element.

flashcards.front: In Randomized Select, when do you stop recursion?
flashcards.back: When the pivot index equals k-1 (for 0-based indexing), meaning the k-th smallest element is found.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of Randomized Select?
flashcards.back: O(log n) expected due to recursion depth, but O(n) worst-case for the call stack.

flashcards.front: What is the advantage of Randomized Select over sorting the entire array?
flashcards.back: It finds the k-th element in O(n) average time vs O(n log n) for sorting, which is much faster when k is small relative to n.

flashcards.front: How does Randomized Select handle the case when k is 1 (finding minimum)?
flashcards.back: It performs a single partition and returns the smallest element, similar to finding the minimum in one pass.

flashcards.front: What happens in Randomized Select if pivot index > k-1?
flashcards.back: The desired element lies in the left partition, so we recurse into the left part with the same k.

flashcards.front: What happens in Randomized Select if pivot index < k-1?
flashcards.back: The desired element lies in the right partition, so we recurse into the right part with k' = k - pivot_index.

flashcards.front: Is Randomized Select a stable algorithm?
flashcards.back: No, it is not stable - the partitioning step can change the relative order of equal elements.

flashcards.front: What is the best-case scenario for Randomized Select?
flashcards.back: O(n) when the pivot always splits the array into two equal halves, similar to the average case.
